claims.claim_text: An early-2023 European e-commerce company handling billions in transaction volume hit AKS networking trouble during a migration.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: In early 2023, a massive European e-commerce organization handling billions in transaction volume embarked on an ambitious migration.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In a large AKS migration, an organization can discover too late that an early networking choice no longer fits production scale.
claims.rationale: This named-and-timed incident setup is not attributable to a real public source and should be converted to a generic teaching scenario.

claims.claim_text: A production promo event caused intermittent 5-second inter-service delays after the system grew to 85 microservices.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: During a high-traffic promotional event, the platform began suffering from intermittent, seemingly inexplicable 5-second delays on inter-service API calls.
claims.suggested_rewrite: At higher service counts and traffic levels, an undersized networking model can surface as intermittent cross-service latency and timeouts.
claims.rationale: The precise outage timing and latency figure are presented as a real incident but are unsourced.

claims.claim_text: AKS networking could not be changed in place, forcing a full rebuild to Azure CNI Overlay.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: Because the Container Network Interface (CNI) cannot be changed on a running cluster, the organization was forced to execute a complete cluster rebuild using Azure CNI Overlay.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Because AKS networking migrations are constrained, disruptive, and in some cases irreversible, networking choices are costly to revisit later.
claims.rationale: Current AKS documentation supports forward-only migration to Azure CNI Overlay for eligible clusters, so the blanket claim is outdated.

claims.claim_text: The remediation took three weeks and cost an estimated $350,000.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters
claims.anchor_text: The migration consumed three weeks of engineering effort, caused numerous maintenance windows, and resulted in an estimated $350,000 in lost revenue and engineering costs.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The remediation required substantial engineering effort, maintenance coordination, and business disruption.
claims.rationale: These precise cost and duration figures are unsourced incident details.

claims.claim_text: With kubenet, nodes get VNet IPs while pods use a different address space, and inter-node pod traffic relies on UDRs/IP forwarding rather than direct pod addressing.
claims.span_hint: section: Kubenet: The Simple (but Limited) Choice
claims.rationale: Microsoft's kubenet documentation directly describes node IP assignment, separate pod addressing, NAT, and UDR/IP forwarding.

claims.claim_text: Kubenet has a 400-route UDR ceiling, adds an extra network hop, supports Calico but not Azure network policies, and does not support Windows node pools.
claims.span_hint: section: Kubenet: The Simple (but Limited) Choice
claims.rationale: These limits and support boundaries are explicitly listed in the AKS kubenet documentation.

claims.claim_text: Azure CNI flat networking assigns pods routable IPs from the VNet so external resources in the network can reach pods directly.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure CNI: Direct VNet Integration
claims.rationale: Microsoft's AKS CNI overview directly contrasts flat networking with overlay networking and describes direct pod exposure.

claims.claim_text: Traditional Azure CNI consumes IPs based on max pods per node, defaults to 30 max pods in the legacy model, and dynamic pod subnet allocation improves IP utilization by separating pod and node subnets.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure CNI: Direct VNet Integration
claims.rationale: The dynamic IP allocation documentation explicitly states the legacy default of 30 and explains the pod-subnet model and improved utilization.

claims.claim_text: Azure CNI Overlay assigns VNet IPs only to nodes, gives pods IPs from a private CIDR, and does not provide direct external pod IP reachability.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure CNI Overlay: Best of Both Worlds
claims.rationale: Microsoft's AKS CNI overview and overlay docs directly describe node-only VNet IP usage and hidden pod IPs in overlay mode.

claims.claim_text: Compared with kubenet, Azure CNI Overlay supports up to 5,000 nodes and 250 pods per node, removes UDR complexity, and supports Azure network policies, Calico, Cilium, and Windows Server node pools.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure CNI Overlay: Best of Both Worlds
claims.rationale: The AKS overlay comparison table directly provides these current scale and support values.

claims.claim_text: Azure CNI Powered by Cilium uses eBPF in the Linux kernel and AKS clusters created with it do not use kube-proxy.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure CNI Powered by Cilium: The Future
claims.rationale: The AKS Cilium documentation explicitly says the data plane uses eBPF and that AKS clusters with Cilium do not use kube-proxy.

claims.claim_text: Cilium provides O(1) lookups, scales cleanly from 10 to 100,000 services, and cuts latency by more than 60% on clusters with 5,000+ services.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure CNI Powered by Cilium: The Future; Did You Know? item 1
claims.anchor_text: On clusters with over 5,000 services, this difference can reduce service routing latency by more than 60%.
claims.suggested_rewrite: At large service counts, Cilium's eBPF-based service handling can improve routing efficiency compared with iptables-based approaches.
claims.rationale: The current allowlisted AKS documentation supports performance and scalability benefits, but not these exact complexity and percentage claims.

claims.claim_text: Cilium on AKS can enforce Layer 7 HTTP/gRPC policies out of the box.
claims.span_hint: section: Cilium Network Policies: L7-Aware Security
claims.anchor_text: Cilium, powered by eBPF, understands HTTP paths, gRPC methods, and DNS queries.
claims.suggested_rewrite: On AKS, Cilium can enforce HTTP, gRPC, and other Layer 7 policies when Advanced Container Networking Services (ACNS) is enabled.
claims.rationale: Current AKS docs place Layer 7 Cilium policies behind ACNS, so the unconditional statement is incomplete.

claims.claim_text: Cilium on AKS supports DNS/FQDN-based egress filtering without qualification.
claims.span_hint: section: Cilium Network Policies: L7-Aware Security
claims.anchor_text: Crucially, Cilium supports DNS-based egress filtering.
claims.suggested_rewrite: On AKS, Cilium supports FQDN-based egress filtering when Advanced Container Networking Services (ACNS) is enabled.
claims.rationale: AKS documentation makes FQDN filtering an ACNS-gated capability.

claims.claim_text: Azure CNI Powered by Cilium supports Windows nodes in preview.
claims.span_hint: section: The Decision Matrix table
claims.rationale: The current AKS Cilium documentation states Azure CNI Powered by Cilium is available only for Linux and not for Windows.

claims.claim_text: Azure Network Policy Manager enforces policies with iptables on Linux and HNS ACL policies on Windows, and it is limited to L3/L4-style enforcement.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure Network Policy Manager (Azure NPM)
claims.rationale: The AKS network policy documentation directly states the enforcement mechanisms and feature differences between engines.

claims.claim_text: Calico provides cluster-wide GlobalNetworkPolicy resources beyond standard Kubernetes NetworkPolicy.
claims.span_hint: section: Calico: The Ecosystem Standard
claims.rationale: This is real and best backed by Calico's primary documentation, but that domain is outside the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: AGIC watches Kubernetes ingress state, translates it into ARM configuration for Azure Application Gateway, and provides external WAF/TLS/autoscaling capabilities on Standard_v2 or WAF_v2.
claims.span_hint: section: Application Gateway Ingress Controller (AGIC)
claims.rationale: The AGIC overview directly documents the ARM translation model, supported SKUs, autoscaling, TLS, and WAF integration.

claims.claim_text: The AKS application routing add-on creates the ingress class `webapprouting.kubernetes.azure.com` and supports certificate integration through the Key Vault certificate URI annotation.
claims.span_hint: section: NGINX Ingress (Web Application Routing Add-on)
claims.rationale: Microsoft's application routing documentation explicitly shows the ingress class and Key Vault TLS annotation.

claims.claim_text: The AGIC versus NGINX gRPC support row is a stable current comparison: AGIC is limited and managed NGINX has full support.
claims.span_hint: section: When to Use Which table
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **gRPC support** | Verify against current Application Gateway documentation | Verify against current managed ingress documentation |
claims.rationale: The current allowlisted documentation does not cleanly support this simplified product-comparison row for AGIC versus the managed add-on.

claims.claim_text: AKS private clusters place the API server behind Azure Private Link with a private endpoint and rely on private DNS for resolution inside the network.
claims.span_hint: section: Private Clusters and Private Link: Hiding the API Server
claims.rationale: The AKS private cluster documentation directly describes Private Link, private endpoints, and private DNS behavior.

claims.claim_text: Private clusters force all CI/CD access to use self-hosted agents inside the VNet.
claims.span_hint: section: Private Clusters and Private Link: Hiding the API Server
claims.anchor_text: You are forced to deploy self-hosted agents within your VNet.
claims.suggested_rewrite: You typically need self-hosted agents or another private access path; for some operational tasks, `az aks command invoke` can be an alternative.
claims.rationale: Microsoft explicitly documents self-hosted-agent limits for Azure DevOps hosted agents and also documents `az aks command invoke` as an alternative access path.

claims.claim_text: Azure API Management can be deployed in internal VNet mode so its endpoints are accessible only within the VNet.
claims.span_hint: section: API Management Integration
claims.rationale: Microsoft's API Management internal-VNet documentation directly supports this deployment model.

claims.claim_text: A NAT Gateway completely eliminates SNAT exhaustion.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure NAT Gateway
claims.suggested_rewrite: This greatly reduces the risk of SNAT exhaustion by providing a much larger shared SNAT port inventory and static outbound IPs.
claims.rationale: Microsoft describes NAT Gateway as minimizing or avoiding the usual load-balancer limits, but absolute 'completely eliminates' wording is too strong.

claims.claim_text: Application Gateway WAF v2 costs $325+/month base.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes table
claims.anchor_text: Application Gateway WAF v2 costs $325+/month base. Use web application routing add-on unless you specifically need WAF
claims.suggested_rewrite: Application Gateway adds a separate base hourly cost plus usage-based charges, so use the web application routing add-on unless you specifically need Application Gateway features such as WAF.
claims.rationale: The exact monthly number varies by region and pricing snapshot, but the lesson that Application Gateway adds separate cost is real and citable.

claims.claim_text: AKS Private Link costs nothing beyond standard cluster pricing.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? item 3
claims.anchor_text: AKS Private Link costs nothing beyond the standard cluster pricing.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Private clusters change your cost profile mainly through network connectivity, DNS, and private-access operational requirements.
claims.rationale: This is a brittle pricing statement and the pedagogically important point is the operational overhead, not a blanket no-cost assertion.

claims.claim_text: The 250-pod AKS maximum is specifically an Azure VMSS limitation rather than a platform support boundary stated by AKS.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? item 2
claims.anchor_text: This is an Azure VMSS limitation, not a Kubernetes one.
claims.suggested_rewrite: In AKS, the supported maximum number of pods per node is 250.
claims.rationale: The current allowlisted AKS docs clearly state the supported AKS maximum, but do not directly support this causal attribution.

claims.claim_text: Most teams find 110 pods per node optimal for Azure CNI.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? item 2
claims.anchor_text: However, most teams find that 110 (the default for Azure CNI) is optimal.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The practical `maxPods` setting depends on workload density, node size, and IP planning.
claims.rationale: This is presented as a broad empirical conclusion without an authoritative source.

claims.claim_text: Azure Firewall can centrally filter outbound traffic by FQDN for authorized destinations.
claims.span_hint: section: Azure Firewall for Centralized Egress
claims.rationale: Microsoft's Azure Firewall documentation directly describes FQDN filtering for outbound traffic.

further_reading.title: Overview of Azure CNI Overlay networking in AKS
further_reading.why_relevant: Best current Microsoft reference for overlay architecture, scale limits, and the kubenet comparison.

further_reading.title: Configure Azure CNI Powered by Cilium in AKS
further_reading.why_relevant: Documents current Cilium support boundaries, kube-proxy behavior, and AKS-specific limitations.

further_reading.title: Create a private AKS cluster
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers Private Link, private DNS behavior, and operational constraints for private control-plane access.

notes: Per task rules, quiz prompts, exercise steps, code blocks, and Mermaid diagrams were not seeded. Several current-staleness issues in prose were handled as rewrites rather than citations, especially the fabricated opening incident, ACNS prerequisites for managed Cilium L7/FQDN features, the outdated Windows-support row for Cilium, and unstable pricing/performance numbers.
